era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is spreading rapidly across Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is at its territorial apex; the Spanish conquest of the Americas is accelerating; the Ming Dynasty is stable; and the Reformation-Catholic divide is hardening into institutional conflict.

events.title: First Kappel War (Peace of Zurich)
events.description: The First Kappel War (June–July 1529) was a brief military conflict between Protestant Zurich under Huldrych Zwingli and Catholic Swiss cantons. The war ended with the Peace of Zurich (29 June 1529), which established a territorial division of Switzerland along religious lines. Each canton was allowed to choose its own religion, but the Catholic cantons retained control over common territories. The peace was fragile and would collapse within two years, leading to the Second Kappel War (1531). The First Kappel War marked the first military conflict of the Reformation era and demonstrated the depth of religious division in Switzerland.
events.certainty_note: The war's dates and the Peace of Zurich are well-documented in contemporary Swiss records and Bullinger's near-contemporary account.

events.title: Siege of Buda by Ottoman Forces
events.description: Ottoman forces under Süleyman the Magnificent besieged Buda (modern Budapest) from May to September 1529. The siege was part of Süleyman's campaign to consolidate Ottoman control over Hungary following the Ottoman victory at the Battle of Mohács (1526). Although the Ottomans failed to capture the city, which was defended by Habsburg forces, the siege demonstrated Ottoman military power and led to the partition of Hungary between Ottoman and Habsburg control. The failure to capture Buda was one of the few major Ottoman setbacks of Süleyman's reign, but it did not prevent Ottoman expansion into Hungary.
key_figures: Ferdinand I (Holy Roman Emperor, Habsburg claimant to Hungary)

sources.name: Ottoman court records and campaign accounts

events.certainty_note: The siege dates and outcome are well-documented in Ottoman, Habsburg, and Hungarian sources.

events.title: Fall of Thomas Wolsey from Power
events.description: Thomas Wolsey, Cardinal and Lord Chancellor of England, fell from power in 1529 after failing to secure a papal annulment of Henry VIII's marriage to Catherine of Aragon. Wolsey had been the most powerful man in England for nearly two decades, but his inability to deliver the annulment led to his disgrace. He was stripped of his offices and arrested for treason. Wolsey's fall marked a turning point in English politics, opening the way for Thomas Cromwell's rise and accelerating the English Reformation. Wolsey died in November 1530 while under arrest.
key_figures: Pope Clement VII (refused to grant annulment)

sources.name: Letters and State Papers of Henry VIII
Cavendish, George. Life of Cardinal Wolsey (1557)

events.certainty_note: Wolsey's fall from power is well-documented in contemporary letters and near-contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Led to rise of Thomas Cromwell (1530s–1540)

events.title: Treaty of Barcelona (Charles V and Pope Clement VII)
events.description: The Treaty of Barcelona (June 1529) was signed between Charles V (Holy Roman Emperor and King of Spain) and Pope Clement VII. The treaty ended the conflict between the Emperor and the Pope that had resulted from the Sack of Rome (1527). Under the treaty, Charles V agreed to restore papal territories and to protect the Pope from Ottoman and French threats. In return, the Pope agreed to crown Charles V as Holy Roman Emperor (which occurred in 1530 at the Diet of Augsburg). The treaty also addressed the question of Henry VIII's marriage annulment, which the Pope continued to refuse. The treaty represented a reconciliation between the Emperor and the Papacy after years of conflict.
key_figures: Francis I (King of France, rival to Charles V)
events.certainty_note: The treaty is a primary text and is well-documented in diplomatic correspondence.
cross_references: Led to Charles V's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor (1530)

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's Death and Jiajing Emperor's Accession (Ming Dynasty)
events.description: Note: The Zhengde Emperor died in 1521, not 1529. In 1529, the Jiajing Emperor (r. 1521–1567) was consolidating his authority after a period of regency and eunuch influence. The Jiajing era was marked by administrative reforms, military campaigns against Mongol incursions, and the rise of the Grand Secretariat. In 1529, the court was dealing with persistent piracy along the coast (the Wokou raids) and managing tributary relations. The year falls within a period of relative stability after the tumultuous Zhengde reign.
key_figures: Yan Song (Grand Secretary, rising to prominence)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative records are well-preserved and provide clear documentation of the Jiajing Emperor's reign.

events.title: Pizarro's Expedition Explores the Peruvian Coast
events.description: Francisco Pizarro's expedition, which had begun in 1527, continued exploring the Peruvian coast in 1529. The expedition was gathering intelligence on the Inca Empire and recruiting indigenous allies. Pizarro had received a royal charter from the Spanish Crown to conquer Peru. The expedition was moving southward along the coast, encountering Inca settlements and learning about the wealth and organization of the Inca Empire. This reconnaissance phase would culminate in Pizarro's march into the highlands and his encounter with Atahualpa in 1532.

sources.name: Pizarro, Francisco. Letters to the Spanish Crown
Cieza de León, Pedro. Crónica del Perú (1553)

events.certainty_note: The expedition's general timeline is well-documented, though specific events in 1529 are less precisely dated than major encounters.
cross_references: Part of Spanish conquest of Inca Empire (1532–1572)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the First Kappel War is sometimes overstated; it was a brief conflict that did not resolve the underlying religious tensions, as evidenced by the Second Kappel War just two years later. The Treaty of Barcelona's significance is debated; some scholars argue it was more symbolic than substantive in resolving the Emperor-Pope conflict. The Jiajing Emperor's reign is sometimes portrayed as more stable than it actually was; internal court conflicts and eunuch influence continued to be significant.
historiographic_note: 1529 is well-documented for European history due to abundant primary sources (treaties, diplomatic correspondence, chronicles). Ottoman history is recorded in court records and diplomatic correspondence. Chinese history is documented through the Ming Shilu. The Spanish conquest of the Americas is documented in Spanish primary sources and later compilations, though indigenous perspectives are mediated through Spanish chroniclers. Western-centric bias is present; Ottoman and Asian sources are less abundant in Western archives.

graph_edges.note: The fragile Peace of Zurich collapsed, leading to the Second Kappel War in 1531
The Siege of Buda was a consequence of the Ottoman victory at Mohács in 1526
Wolsey's fall from power in 1529 led to his death in 1530
The Treaty of Barcelona resolved the conflict resulting from the Sack of Rome in 1527
